analysis:
- Top performers share the same high-level structure: deterministic Pareto prefilter → cheap per-point proxy (box-volume ± few-weight scalarization) → compact O(k)-shortlist → cached singleton HVs → CELF-style lazy greedy (timestamping) → bounded 1-for-1 local swaps → deterministic padding/tie-breaks.  
- Efficiency wins come from vectorized proxies, O(k)-sized shortlists, reusing cached singleton (and selective pairwise) HVs, and tight upper‑bound pruning so exact HV calls are infrequent.  
- Weaker variants either (a) use expensive full-set HV recomputations (e.g., brute-force removal loops), (b) have oversized shortlists or large HV budgets, or (c) lack lazy evaluation / caching, causing many redundant HV calls.  
- Parameter choices matter: shortlist scale, number of weight vectors, and swap iteration caps trade accuracy for runtime. Deterministic tie-breakers and small pairwise caches improve repeatability and early marginal estimates.  
- Practical pitfalls observed: recomputing current HV from scratch in inner loops, evaluating full removal contributions, and unchecked randomization increase cost and variability.
experience: Design pipelines that (1) Pareto-filter, (2) rank with vectorized box+scalar proxies to build an O(k) shortlist, (3) precompute singleton HVs, (4) run CELF lazy greedy with tight upper bounds and an HV-call budget, then (5) do a small bounded swap pass. Cache pairwise HVs only when shortlist ≤ ~40 and keep all steps deterministic.
current_reflection: Design pipelines that (1) Pareto-filter, (2) rank with vectorized box+scalar proxies to build an O(k) shortlist, (3) precompute singleton HVs, (4) run CELF lazy greedy with tight upper bounds and an HV-call budget, then (5) do a small bounded swap pass. Cache pairwise HVs only when shortlist ≤ ~40 and keep all steps deterministic.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.

Design next heuristics by enforcing an O(k) shortlist, always cache singleton HVs, use CELF lazy evaluation with timestamps, apply cheap box-volume upper bounds to prune candidates, enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists, cap HV calls and swap iterations, and prefer deterministic stable ordering. These focused changes yield most HV-quality gain per expensive HV call.

Prioritize reducing exact HV calls: build a high-precision O(k) shortlist with cheap proxies, cache singleton (and small pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates and box‑bound pruning, then run a tiny deterministic swap pass. Add an explicit HV-call budget with graceful fallback approximations and vectorized nondomination/precomputation to get consistent, fast, and high-quality k‑subsets.
bad_reflections: For the next generation, combine: adaptive shortlist sizing (scale with pareto size and k), CELF lazy greedy with cached singletons + selective pairwise HVs, and tight upper-bound pruning (box-vol or scalar HVC) to avoid useless HV calls. Add an HV-call budget, incremental HV updates, and prioritize swap candidates via proxy gains or diversity. Vectorize dominance checks and weight computations; keep all randomness seeded and tie-break deterministically.

Always budget and minimize HV calls: prioritize vectorized proxies for aggressive pruning, cache singleton HVs, compute pairwise HVs only for very small shortlists, and use CELF lazy timestamps to amortize updates. Favor deterministic, O(N log N) shortlist construction and bounded, incremental swaps. Optimize hot loops with numpy and sets to avoid Python overhead; add an explicit HV-call budget and conservative upper bounds to maximize runtime-quality trade-offs.

Favor a pipeline that (1) vectorizes cheap proxies to form an adaptive O(k) shortlist, (2) precompute singleton HVs, (3) run CELF lazy greedy with timestamps and cheap upper‑bound pruning, (4) limit exact HV calls with an explicit budget and selective pairwise cache, and (5) finish with a tiny bounded swap. Optimize data structures (sets, arrays) and avoid repeated full HV recomputations.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: Pareto-filter, O(k) shortlist, vectorized proxies, singleton HV cache, CELF lazy (timestamps), box upper bounds, HV-call budget, bounded swap, deterministic, pairwise cache ≤40
- Advice: Deterministically Pareto-filter then vectorize box+scalar proxies to form an O(k) shortlist; precompute singleton HVs; run CELF lazy greedy with tight box bounds and an explicit HV-call budget; enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists and finish with a tiny bounded swap pass.
- Avoid: Nested or repeated full HV recomputations, unbounded HV calls, non-deterministic tie-breaking, and enabling pairwise caches on large shortlists or hot Python loops that trigger HV overhead.
- Explanation: Cheap proxies, singleton caching and lazy updates give most HV gain per exact call; bounds + a budget make runtime predictable and a small swap repairs local suboptimality.
